response:
You can definitely achieve your goal without setting environment variables manually.
The Flask application factory pattern allows for flexible configuration in many ways.
You might want to examine how the CLI invokes your application code internally.
There is a specific hook called `click` that manages the command line interface.
You can detect the invocation context by checking the current command name.
This approach avoids the need for external environment variable setup entirely.
Imagine a scenario where you run `flask my_command` without any extra flags.
The system can automatically detect this specific command and adjust behavior.
You could create a wrapper function that checks if the call is from the CLI.
This wrapper would then call your `create_app` with a special flag set.
The flag could be a simple boolean named `is_cli_mode` for clarity.
This boolean would be passed directly to your factory function during initialization.
Your factory function can then branch logic based on this specific parameter value.
If the value is true, you load a different configuration or skip certain steps.
This method keeps your command line usage clean and simple for end users.
You do not need to remember to type `FLASK_MY_VARIABLE=x` every single time.
The logic to distinguish modes lives entirely within your application code structure.
This design pattern is quite common in larger enterprise Flask projects today.
It provides a robust way to handle different execution contexts seamlessly.
You can also use the `app.cli` object to register commands with custom parameters.
These parameters can be passed directly to the command function itself.
However, modifying the app creation logic is often more central and effective.
Consider using a context variable to store the mode during the request lifecycle.
This ensures that every part of your app knows exactly how it was invoked.
You might also want to look at the `FLASK_APP` environment variable behavior.
Sometimes the shell script that runs Flask can be modified to set defaults.
But the internal detection method you asked about is the most elegant solution.
It requires no changes to how your team or users type commands in the terminal.
